event_name: Mythology Bingo with Togas and Tales
blurb:
Bingo, but make it mythology. Togas and Tales is bringing their Mythology Bingo program to Mountain View Regional Library on Monday afternoon, and if you've got a kid who spent last summer deep in Percy Jackson or just genuinely likes weird trivia, this is a free Monday with a purpose.

It runs from 2 to 3pm, which is an easy add to the afternoon without eating the whole day. The library is right on Sandy Plains Road, so it's about as convenient as it gets for East Cobb families.

Free, local, and genuinely a little different. Worth circling if you've got the right kid in your house.
scoring_notes: Fits families with younger kids or teens, which is part of the East Cobb audience mix. Mythology theme is more specific and interesting than generic summer programming. Free and hyperlocal. Scores lower on demographic fit and audience match because it's a narrower fit (younger kids) in a newsletter that also serves many empty nesters.

blurb:
No cover, two pianists, and a crowd that gets louder as the night goes on. Dueling Pianos at Park Bench in The Battery runs Saturday night from 7 to 11pm, and it's the kind of show that works whether you're planning a big night out or just want to wander in and see what happens.

General seating fills up fast, so if you want a spot, they do take table reservations. Groups and celebrations are explicitly welcomed, which makes this an easy pick if you've got a reason to celebrate or just want to manufacture one.

No tickets to buy in advance, no admission at the door. Just show up, find a seat, and let the crowd sing along to songs you forgot you knew.
scoring_notes: Dueling pianos is a crowd-pleasing format that fits the 40+ demo well. No cover makes it low-friction. The Battery is familiar territory for East Cobb. Uniqueness is moderate since this appears to be a recurring format at the venue rather than a one-time event, but it's still a strong Saturday night option.

event_name: Jackass: Best and Last Fan Event
blurb:
Johnny Knoxville and the crew are back for what they're calling the last one. Jackass: Best and Last hits the big screen Thursday night at Springs Cinema & Taphouse, and the 7pm opening night show comes with a limited edition collector's print while supplies last.

This isn't a nostalgia bait cash grab. It's new stunts mixed with the greatest hits from 25 years of absolute nonsense, and Springs Cinema is the right venue for it. Taphouse in the name means you can have a drink in your hand while you watch a man get hit by a shopping cart. That's the evening.

If you were a Jackass fan in your 20s and 30s, this one is squarely aimed at you. Opening night, collector's print, a bar. Go.
scoring_notes: The 42 median age for East Cobb puts this audience squarely in Jackass's original demographic from the early 2000s. Opening night fan event with a limited collector's print adds uniqueness. Springs Cinema is close enough to East Cobb. Skews a bit more toward adults than families, but teens would enjoy it too.

blurb:
Sunday afternoon, your own bar station, three cocktails you built yourself. That's how ASW Distillery runs their monthly cocktail class, and it's a better Sunday plan than whatever you were already considering.

One of their lead mixologists walks the group through shaking, stirring, and building three cocktails using ASW's award-winning spirits. You're not watching someone demonstrate. You're doing it yourself, with all the tools in front of you. It runs 90 minutes, which is exactly the right length for this kind of thing.

This is a fun one to do with a partner or a small group of friends. It's the Battery, so you can make a full afternoon out of it. Spots are limited, so book ahead.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for the 40+ adult demo with $118K household income. Hands-on experience format beats a standard bar outing for this audience. Monthly means it recurs, but the specific June 28 date is what matters here. Slightly lower audience match because it skews more toward couples and pairs than families.

event_name: Bourbon & Brisket Festival at The Battery Atlanta
venue: The Plaza at Battery Atlanta, Atlanta
blurb:
Eight hours of BBQ, bourbon, live music, and games at The Battery. That's the whole pitch, and honestly it doesn't need much more than that.

The Bourbon & Brisket Festival is in its third year, which means the kinks are worked out and the lineup is dialed in. Premium bourbon tastings, full pop-up bars, and enough smoked meat to make you deeply regret skipping breakfast. Live music and DJs carry it through the afternoon and into the evening.

It runs noon to 8pm on a Saturday, which is basically a perfect summer day with a very clear agenda. If you've been looking for a reason to get out to The Battery this month, this is a SOLID one.
scoring_notes: Great fit for the 40+ crowd with disposable income. BBQ and bourbon skews adult, which matches the empty nester and established family demo. The Battery is a familiar destination for East Cobb residents. Third annual means it's proven but not novel; uniqueness docked slightly compared to a one-time event.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs. St. Louis Cardinals
blurb:
The Braves close out June hosting the Cardinals at Truist Park, first pitch at 7:15. If you've been meaning to get out to a game this summer and haven't pulled the trigger yet, this is a midweek option that doesn't require you to fight a weekend crowd.

Truist Park is basically East Cobb's backyard at this point. Grab tickets, get there early enough to walk The Battery, grab dinner, and be in your seat for the first pitch. Wednesday night baseball in the summer is genuinely one of the better ways to use a weeknight.

Tickets are available now. Don't wait until Tuesday.
scoring_notes: Braves games are a near-universal fit for East Cobb's demographic. Proximity to Truist Park is a major factor here. The Cardinals are a marquee opponent with a historic rivalry feel. Uniqueness is moderate since it's one game in a long season, but relevance to this specific audience is very high.

event_name: Scene on the Green: End of the Line Concert
venue: The Art Place at Mountain View, Marietta
blurb:
Free live bluegrass under the stars, right in the neighborhood. Cobb County Parks is putting on Scene on the Green at The Art Place on Mountain View Road, and End of the Line brings the high-energy music.

This is the kind of evening that's easy to talk yourself out of and easy to love once you're there. Pack a blanket, grab something to eat beforehand, and settle in for 90 minutes of live music. It starts at 7:30pm when the heat has backed off a little, which is all you really need to know.

Free admission, close to home, and something actually happening in your backyard. Worth the trip.
scoring_notes: Hyper-local, free, and walkable for many East Cobb residents. Bluegrass skews well toward the 42 median age. Not a weekly recurring event, it's a specific summer series installment with a named act. Strong neighborhood relevance boosts audience match even if the uniqueness is moderate.

venue: Jim R. Miller Park & Event Center, Marietta
blurb:
This one only happens once. Cobb County is turning 250 years old and throwing a community celebration at Jim R. Miller Park to mark the occasion. You're not going to get another shot at this particular party.

The event runs from 5pm to 10pm, which means you've got a full evening of it. Bring the family, bring the neighbors, grab some food, and be part of something that's genuinely historic for this area. It's the kind of night where you're glad you didn't skip it.

No admission, no tickets to hunt down. Just show up.
scoring_notes: A once-in-a-lifetime local milestone. East Cobb families, empty nesters, and longtime residents all have a stake in this. Free admission removes any friction. The 5-10pm window is ideal for the audience. Uniqueness score is as high as it gets: 250 years is not a recurring event.
